headline: Illinois Opens Menopause Care to Thousands of Doctors
meta_description: Illinois becomes first state to embed menopause training into existing medical education requirements, expanding access across the state.
summary_short: Illinois passed a groundbreaking law letting healthcare providers train in menopause care—without adding new requirements. It's reshaping how medicine addresses women's…
body:
When Illinois Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton held financial wellness conversations with women across the state, she expected to hear about rent and wages. Instead, menopause came up repeatedly—its toll on work, money, and daily life.

That direct feedback led to something unprecedented. In May, Illinois unanimously passed a law allowing licensed healthcare professionals to count specialized menopause and perimenopause training toward their required implicit bias awareness hours. No new mandates. No additional hours. The training simply becomes an option within education providers already do.

"When I think about a state of almost 13 million people and half of the state being women—200 people being certified just wasn't enough," Stratton said, referring to Illinois's shortage of menopause-certified physicians.

The numbers reveal why action was urgent. Seventy percent of women seeking menopause care receive no treatment. Black and Latina women experience more severe symptoms yet receive hormone therapy less often. Globally, menopause-related productivity losses exceed $150 billion annually.

Starting January 1, 2027, the course will cover hormone therapy basics, FDA-approved non-hormonal options, and culturally responsive care. Pauline Maki, director of the Center on Health, Awareness and Research on Menopause at the University of Illinois College of Medicine, designed the training.

Illinois is first, but not alone. Ten states and Washington, D.C., have now passed menopause legislation. Stratton plans to take this work federal after winning her Senate race.
